beats.prompt: Warm-up recall from Expected Value L1. Match each result to its value.

pairs.left: A fair bet is worth its…
An option is a bet whose payoff…

feedback.correct: E[X]=7/2 is the fair price; a bet on a kink bends at the strike — that's what makes a call not a forward.
hints: Both came from Expected Value (ev1-recall/ev1-win).
Die mean = (1+2+3+4+5+6)/6 = 7/2; a bet is worth its expectation.
7/2; the option payoff bends at the strike K.

beats.prompt: Long call K=100. At expiry S_T=70. What is the call worth at expiry?

-30.note: Let's test it — that treats the call like a forward (an obligation). A call is a right: when the stock is cheaper you simply walk away. The payoff is max(S−K,0) — floored at 0, never −30. You never exercise into a loss.

0.note: Good instinct — exercising would mean overpaying, so you don't. Payoff = max(70−100,0) = 0. The downside is clamped at the strike — that's the kink.

-5.note: Let's test it — that's profit (payoff minus the premium), not the contract's payoff. Today we read the payoff at expiry, which is 0 here. The premium and P&L come later (pricing, L2+). Payoff is always ≥ 0; only profit can go negative.

beats.prompt: Toggle long-call ↔ long-put. Drag the S_T marker along the x-axis and read the payoff live — watch the hockey-stick curve.

hero.structuralReadout: Hockey-stick curve — flat at 0 below K=100, rises 45° above. The kink is the whole idea.

feedback.correct: Both payoffs are 0 below (or above) the strike — the kink at K is the whole idea. A straight line would be a forward.
hints: Tap the CALL/PUT toggle and drag the markS marker left and right.
Move markS below K, at K, above K — read 0, 0, and positive respectively.
Below its strike the call pays 0; above it pays S_T−K. The kink is at K=100.

beats.prompt: The call payoff formula — three lenses.

interaction.title: Payoff = max(·,0) — the kink at K
interaction.body:
① Definition — A call gives you a right, not an obligation: exercise only when favorable. Payoff = max(S_T−K, 0), always ≥ 0, since you walk away from a losing exercise.

② The (·)⁺ floor — The clamp max(·,0) creates the kink at K. Intrinsic value is floored at 0; below the strike, intrinsic = 0, never negative. Call: (S−K)⁺; put: (K−S)⁺.

③ Intrinsic vs time value — At expiry, option value = intrinsic = max(·,0). Before expiry the option carries extra time value (the remaining chance to end up in the money). Pricing the time value is deferred — today we read the contract.

feedback.correct: Payoff = max(·,0) — the floor IS the kink. Pricing is later.
hints: Read the three lenses.
The (·)⁺ clamp is the kink at K — the payoff is never negative.
Continue when ready.

beats.prompt: Formula max(S−K, 0), K=100. Type the call payoff at each expiry spot.

feedback.correct: Yes — max(130−100,0)=30; max(70−100,0)=0. The floor kicks in below K and clamps the payoff to 0.
hints: The payoff needs two pieces — the inner gap and the floor; you have the formula.
max(130−100, 0): inner gap = 30 ≥ 0 so payoff = 30; max(70−100, 0): inner gap = −30 < 0 so payoff = 0 — don't stop at the gap.

beats.prompt: At expiry the stock sits at S_T=80. You hold a long put with K=100 and need its cash value at expiry. Which move gets you there?

Payoff construction.note: Right — at a known expiry spot you evaluate the piecewise-linear payoff: max(100−80,0)=20. No second instrument, no portfolio — payoff construction.

Put–call parity.note: Parity (C−P=S−K·D) ties a call and a put's prices together; here you're handed one option and a known S_T and asked for its cash value. There's no second instrument and nothing to price — reading max(K−S,0) is the move. Save parity for L2, when two prices must agree.

No-arbitrage replication.note: Replication builds a synthetic copy to price an option before expiry. At expiry, with S_T known, you read the payoff straight off the kinked line — no hedge, no replicating portfolio. That's pricing machinery for later lessons.

hints: One move reads a cash value at a known S_T; the other two are for pricing before expiry — which one fits?
The formula is max(K−S_T, 0); nothing else is needed to get the number.
Payoff construction: max(100−80,0)=20.

beats.prompt: Match each payoff expression to its value — call and put at the same strike.

pairs.left: Below its strike, a call's intrinsic value is

feedback.correct: Same method, different costume — both payoffs are max(·,0) of the favorable gap, floored at 0. A below-strike call has 0 intrinsic, never negative.
hints: One is a call (S−K)⁺, one is a put (K−S)⁺ — what's the same?
Call: max(130−100,0)=30; put: max(100−70,0)=30; below-strike call: the gap is negative → floored to 0.

beats.prompt: Long straddle: long call K=100 + long put K=100. At expiry, what is the payoff at S_T=130? At S_T=70?

interaction.scenario: Long straddle: one call leg (K=100) plus one put leg (K=100). Evaluate each leg separately, then add. At S_T=130: call pays max(130−100,0), put pays max(100−130,0). At S_T=70: call pays max(70−100,0), put pays max(100−70,0).

feedback.correct: The straddle is call + put at the same K — you get paid whichever way the stock moves. Payoff = |S_T−100| = 30 both ways.
hints: A straddle has two legs — evaluate each leg separately, then add.
At S_T=130: call pays max(30,0)=30, put pays max(−30,0)=0 → total 30; at S_T=70: call pays 0, put pays 30 → total 30.
30 (both spots, #4).

beats.prompt: Protective put: long stock + long put K=100. At expiry, what is the terminal value at S_T=130? At S_T=80?

interaction.scenario: Protective put: long stock (pays S_T always) + long put K=100 (pays max(100−S_T,0)). Terminal value = S_T + max(100−S_T,0) = max(S_T,100). Evaluate at S_T=130 and S_T=80.

feedback.correct: Long stock + long put = protective put. The put floors the terminal value at K=100, so the total is max(S_T,100) — 130 when the stock wins, 100 when it doesn't.
hints: Add the two payoffs: stock pays S_T always; put pays max(100−S_T,0).
At S_T=130: stock=130, put=max(100−130,0)=0 → total 130; at S_T=80: stock=80, put=max(100−80,0)=20 → total 100.

beats.interviewNote: The straddle (long call + long put, same K) pays |S_T−K| — minimum at the strike, V-shaped. The protective put (long stock + long put K) floors terminal wealth at K via max(S_T,K). Both identities follow from adding kinked max(·,0) lines, no pricing needed.

feedback.correct: Payoff = max(·,0), the kink at K; compose legs, payoffs add; next: parity & no-arbitrage.
hints: Read each recap card.
Every result is one piecewise-linear max(·,0) payoff.
Tap to finish.
